title: Mac and Dennis Buy a Timeshare

jokes.text: You're, like, a berry salesman now or...? No, I'm a distributor, Charlie, for a revolutionary new product that changes lives.
jokes.setup: Charlie's casual dismissal of Dee's obvious MLM scheme
jokes.punchline: Dee's overly serious correction emphasizing 'distributor' like it's prestigious
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Dee's delusional self-importance versus Charlie's blunt honesty. The word 'distributor' delivered with such gravity is absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You're, like, a berry salesman now or...?

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: No, I'm a distributor, Charlie, for a revolutionary new product that changes lives.

jokes.text: Oh, those are my new wrenches. I got those free for sitting through a 20-minute presentation.
jokes.setup: Context suggests these are cheap promotional items from another scam
jokes.punchline: Dee proudly displays her 'free' wrenches like they're valuable prizes
jokes.explanation: Shows how easily Dee gets suckered by free junk. The pride in her voice about free wrenches is perfectly pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Oh, those are my new wrenches. I got those free for sitting through a 20-minute presentation.

jokes.text: Oh, I get it now. It's a scam.
jokes.setup: Charlie immediately recognizing the obvious MLM scheme
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact delivery while Dee remains oblivious
jokes.explanation: Charlie being the voice of reason is inherently funny. His blunt assessment cuts through Dee's delusion perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Oh, I get it now. It's a scam.

jokes.text: Alpacas can survive entire winters on this shit.
jokes.setup: Dee trying to sell the berries with scientific-sounding claims
jokes.punchline: Alpaca survival being her best evidence, delivered with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: The specific absurdity of alpaca survival as a selling point, combined with Dee's earnest delivery, makes this genuinely funny.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Alpacas can survive entire winters on this shit.

jokes.text: Now what kind of superpowers are you talking about? Like, do you survive the winter?
jokes.setup: Dee mentioned 'super berries'
jokes.punchline: Charlie's literal interpretation that winter survival is a superpower
jokes.explanation: Charlie's childlike logic about superpowers is perfectly in character and absurd. His genuine curiosity makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Now what kind of superpowers are you talking about? Like, do you survive the winter?

jokes.text: Because I've survived many winters without these berries.
jokes.setup: Discussion of berries helping survive winter
jokes.punchline: Charlie's obvious observation delivered as if it's profound insight
jokes.explanation: Charlie's unintentionally deflating the sales pitch with basic logic. The earnest delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Because I've survived many winters without these berries.

jokes.text: Where do I put my feet? Wherever you want. I'm gonna put them on the stool. Great. It doesn't matter.
jokes.setup: Fake stress-testing machine setup
jokes.punchline: The pointless focus on feet placement when it literally doesn't matter
jokes.explanation: Shows the arbitrary nature of their fake test. Charlie's overthinking of meaningless details is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Where do I put my feet?

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Wherever you want.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna put them on the stool.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Great. It doesn't matter.

jokes.text: 157 what? Units. Units, dude. Units of what? Units of stress!
jokes.setup: Mac questioning the meaningless measurement
jokes.punchline: Dee's circular non-explanation getting more defensive
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of nonsense. 'Units of stress' is beautifully meaningless pseudoscience delivered with conviction.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: what? Units. Units, dude. Units of what?

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Units of stress!

jokes.text: You're getting scammed, Dee! It's not a scam! You walked into a room that you could have walked out of with a free set of golf club but instead you got got.
jokes.setup: Mac calling out the obvious scam
jokes.punchline: His own poor grammar undermining his wisdom ('golf club' singular)
jokes.explanation: Mac being right about the scam while simultaneously revealing his own susceptibility through poor grammar.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: You're getting scammed, Dee!

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: It's not a scam!

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: You walked into a room that you could have walked out of with a free set of golf club but instead you got got.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, we won't get got though. We gonna get.
jokes.setup: Mac declaring their immunity to scams
jokes.punchline: His confident but grammatically broken declaration
jokes.explanation: Mac's mangled grammar while trying to sound street-smart is perfectly him. 'We gonna get' is instantly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, we won't get got though. We gonna get.

jokes.text: See, Dee, people like us, we don't get got. We go get.
jokes.setup: Following Mac's broken grammar
jokes.punchline: Dennis repeating the same mangled logic more smoothly
jokes.explanation: Dennis adopting Mac's broken logic shows how they enable each other's delusions. The repetition amplifies the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: See, Dee, people like us, we don't get got. We go get.

jokes.text: What are you doing, Charlie?! I'm drinking the berry juice to save my life.
jokes.setup: Charlie apparently drinking the product they're supposed to sell
jokes.punchline: His melodramatic justification for consuming their inventory
jokes.explanation: Charlie's complete misunderstanding of the business model. His life-or-death urgency about berry juice is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: What are you doing, Charlie?!

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I'm drinking the berry juice to save my life.

jokes.text: 'wealth creation through the use of antioxidants, powerful antioxidants' and 'redefine success and change the world.'
jokes.setup: Dee reciting obvious MLM buzzwords
jokes.punchline: The completely hollow corporate speak delivered earnestly
jokes.explanation: Generic MLM parody. Works because Dee believes it, but not particularly creative or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: wealth creation through the use of antioxidants, powerful antioxidants

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: redefine success and change the world.

jokes.text: All those words are, like, stressing me out, Dee. And I can't get more stress... I'm at 157!
jokes.setup: Charlie's stress about business jargon
jokes.punchline: Calling back to the meaningless stress measurement as if it's real
jokes.explanation: Charlie treating the fake measurement as gospel is perfect. The callback works because he's genuinely worried about his 'stress units.'

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: All those words are, like, stressing me out, Dee. And I can't get more stress... I'm at 157!

jokes.text: He said he had some dream or something. And, like, he woke up, he's like, 'Ah, Charlie, I got to get to the playground.' And he shot out of here.
jokes.setup: Frank's mysterious absence
jokes.punchline: The completely inexplicable urgency about getting to a playground
jokes.explanation: Frank's random playground urgency is perfectly bizarre. The casual delivery of this insane behavior makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: He said he had some dream or something. And, like, he woke up, he's like, 'Ah, Charlie, I got to get to the playground.' And he shot out of here.

jokes.text: Frank? What is this? You're stuck? Was this your plan?
jokes.setup: Finding Frank somehow stuck in playground equipment
jokes.punchline: The visual of a grown man trapped in a coil, naked
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual comedy setup. Frank stuck naked in playground equipment is peak Sunny absurdity. The matter-of-fact discovery makes it even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Frank? What is this? You're stuck? Was this your plan?

jokes.text: How did this happen? How does anything happen, Charlie? Move past it, will ya?
jokes.setup: Charlie's reasonable question about Frank's predicament
jokes.punchline: Frank's philosophical deflection while literally stuck naked in a coil
jokes.explanation: Frank's casual dismissal of his insane situation is perfectly in character. 'How does anything happen' is beautifully absurd philosophy from a man in a coil.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: How did this happen?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: How does anything happen, Charlie? Move past it, will ya?

jokes.text: It's a pyramid scheme. It's not a pyramid scheme. It's a reverse funnel system. Turn it upside down. Oh, shit!
jokes.setup: Frank immediately identifying the pyramid scheme
jokes.punchline: Dee's revelation when she literally turns the diagram upside down
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and payoff. The visual gag of literally turning it upside down to reveal the pyramid is brilliant. 'Reverse funnel system' is hilariously corporate.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: It's a pyramid scheme.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: It's not a pyramid scheme.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: It's a reverse funnel system.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Turn it upside down.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Oh, shit!

jokes.text: You're the sucker, okay? You're naked and you're stuck in a coil!
jokes.setup: Frank calling them suckers
jokes.punchline: Dee's perfect comeback highlighting Frank's current predicament
jokes.explanation: Great comeback that highlights the absurdity. Dee finally has the moral high ground because Frank is literally trapped naked.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: You're the sucker, okay? You're naked and you're stuck in a coil!

jokes.text: If we get him out of there, how do we know he's gonna keep helping us? If we get him out of there, he might scam us further.
jokes.setup: The obvious moral choice to help Frank
jokes.punchline: Their calculated decision to keep him trapped for leverage
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold. Their businesslike discussion of keeping Frank trapped shows how awful they are. The logic is simultaneously horrible and sound.

dialogue.line: If we get him out of there, how do we know he's gonna keep helping us? If we get him out of there, he might scam us further.

jokes.text: I say we leave him stuck in there just for a few more days so we can leverage him for business advice... We keep him contained.
jokes.setup: Already deciding to leave Frank trapped
jokes.punchline: Escalating to treating him like a business consultant prisoner
jokes.explanation: The escalation from helping to imprisonment for business advice is perfectly awful. 'Leverage' and 'contained' make it sound corporate.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I say we leave him stuck in there just for a few more days so we can leverage him for business advice... We keep him contained.

jokes.text: It's Saturday coming up. There's gonna be kids here!
jokes.setup: Frank trapped naked in playground equipment
jokes.punchline: The horrifying implication of children finding him
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about the terrible situation Frank has created. The desperation adds to the cringe factor.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: It's Saturday coming up. There's gonna be kids here!

jokes.text: Save it, bozo. We're here for the free golf clubs. You got exactly 20 minutes to do your little song and dance.
jokes.setup: Timeshare salesman trying to engage them
jokes.punchline: Mac's aggressive dismissal while walking into the exact same trap
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for their downfall. Mac's confidence while being completely wrong is classic. 'Little song and dance' is dismissively funny.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Save it, bozo. We're here for the free golf clubs. You got exactly 20 minutes to do your little song and dance.

jokes.text: You fellows strike me more as men of leisure. Obviously. Well, we are.
jokes.setup: Salesman reading them perfectly
jokes.punchline: Mac and Dennis proudly confirming they're exactly the target demographic
jokes.explanation: They immediately fall for the flattery. Their pride in being 'men of leisure' shows how easily manipulated they are.

dialogue.speaker: Harris
dialogue.line: You fellows strike me more as men of leisure. Obviously.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Well, we are.

jokes.text: Been there? Not physically.
jokes.setup: Asked if they've been to Florida
jokes.punchline: Dennis's pretentious non-answer that means nothing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dennis pretension. 'Not physically' implies some deeper connection to Florida that doesn't exist. Hilariously meaningless.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Been there? Not physically.

jokes.text: What happens if we buy a third week and sell that? We're getting paid to vacation!
jokes.setup: The flawed logic of selling weeks for profit
jokes.punchline: Dennis's escalation to the absurd conclusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of flawed logic. Dennis's excitement about 'getting paid to vacation' shows how completely he's been manipulated.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: What happens if we buy a third week and sell that? We're getting paid to vacation!

jokes.text: You got got! You thought we were a bunch of suckers, but guess what? We're not. We just bought three weeks of a time-share.
jokes.setup: Mac thinking they've won
jokes.punchline: His victory declaration while describing exactly how they got scammed
jokes.explanation: Brilliant irony. Mac's triumphant declaration of not being suckers while literally describing how they got suckered. Perfect callback to earlier confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: You got got! You thought we were a bunch of suckers, but guess what? We're not. We just bought three weeks of a time-share.

jokes.text: Nobody gives a shit about free soup, Charlie. People want golf clubs and concert tickets.
jokes.setup: Poor turnout at their MLM presentation
jokes.punchline: Dee's dismissal of Charlie's pathetic incentive
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing how out of touch they are. Free soup as an incentive is perfectly pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Nobody gives a shit about free soup, Charlie. People want golf clubs and concert tickets.

jokes.text: Da'Maniac's here. He's sucked down, like, three bowls of it.
jokes.setup: Discussion of poor turnout for free soup
jokes.punchline: Da'Maniac being the only person excited about free soup
jokes.explanation: Da'Maniac as the only attendee who's actually enjoying the terrible incentive. Sets up his importance later.

dialogue.line: Da'Maniac's here. He's sucked down, like, three bowls of it.

jokes.text: As you can tell by our huge mansion, we're insanely loaded. But it wasn't always this way. We used to be losers, like all of you people.
jokes.setup: Dee's sales presentation in their obvious dive bar
jokes.punchline: Calling it a 'huge mansion' and insulting their audience
jokes.explanation: Perfect delusion. Calling Paddy's a 'huge mansion' while immediately insulting potential customers shows complete incompetence.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: As you can tell by our huge mansion, we're insanely loaded. But it wasn't always this way. We used to be losers, like all of you people.

jokes.text: Yeah, I had to do some reshoots. You are way too hung up on the berries.
jokes.setup: Charlie asking about his missing part in the video
jokes.punchline: Dee treating their amateur video like a Hollywood production
jokes.explanation: 'Reshoots' for their terrible homemade video shows Dee's delusions of professionalism. Also shows their dysfunction.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Yeah, I had to do some reshoots. You are way too hung up on the berries.

jokes.text: I'm Andy, proud owner of my own mountain, where I'm a magical bean farmer.
jokes.setup: Charlie's attempted professional presentation
jokes.punchline: His completely unhinged introduction as a 'magical bean farmer'
jokes.explanation: Pure Charlie absurdity. 'Magical bean farmer' as a business credential is hilariously unhinged. The fake name 'Andy' adds to the chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I'm Andy, proud owner of my own mountain, where I'm a magical bean farmer.

jokes.text: Well, guess what? The Invigaron system is also a pyramid scheme.
jokes.setup: Charlie trying to make a sales pitch
jokes.punchline: Accidentally revealing it's a pyramid scheme as a selling point
jokes.explanation: Brilliant anti-sales pitch. Charlie's complete misunderstanding of what makes something appealing is perfectly wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Well, guess what? The Invigaron system is also a pyramid scheme.

jokes.text: Basically a house of cards where the dickheads at the bottom do all the work, and give all the money to the smart guys up at the top.
jokes.setup: Charlie explaining pyramid schemes
jokes.punchline: His brutally honest description presented as a sales pitch
jokes.explanation: Charlie accurately describing the scam while trying to sell it. The casual use of 'dickheads' in a sales pitch is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Basically a house of cards where the dickheads at the bottom do all the work, and give all the money to the smart guys up at the top.

jokes.text: You saw the video. Was the video not clear? The video was pretty clear, Charlie.
jokes.setup: Ben saying he doesn't understand what he's buying
jokes.punchline: Ben politely agreeing their terrible video was clear
jokes.explanation: Ben's politeness contrasted with the obviously terrible video they made. Shows how confused everyone is.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: You saw the video. Was the video not clear?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: The video was pretty clear, Charlie.

jokes.text: Thank you for this business opportunity. I will not let you down. Sir, you need to pay for the product, you can't just take it out of here.
jokes.setup: Da'Maniac's earnest acceptance of the opportunity
jokes.punchline: Him just stealing the product while giving a professional speech
jokes.explanation: Da'Maniac's formal business language while committing theft. The contrast between his professionalism and his actions is great.

dialogue.speaker: Da'Maniac
dialogue.line: Thank you for this business opportunity. I will not let you down.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Sir, you need to pay for the product, you can't just take it out of here.

jokes.text: 107. Hey, man, that's pretty good. Don't... Healthy guy over here. Charlie, no, that's terrible.
jokes.setup: Another stress test reading
jokes.punchline: Charlie and Dee's completely opposite reactions to the same meaningless number
jokes.explanation: Shows they have no idea how their fake machine works. Charlie's congratulations followed by Dee's panic is good chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: 107. Hey, man, that's pretty good. Don't... Healthy guy over here.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Charlie, no, that's terrible.

jokes.text: Is it a sexual thing? No, just get me the hell out! It doesn't matter! Matters to me; I don't think I can move past it.
jokes.setup: Mac's inappropriate question about Frank being stuck in playground equipment
jokes.punchline: Mac's inability to focus on helping without understanding the sexual angle
jokes.explanation: Mac's obsession with the sexual implications while Frank desperately needs help. His priorities are perfectly wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Is it a sexual thing?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: No, just get me the hell out!

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: It doesn't matter!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Matters to me; I don't think I can move past it.

jokes.text: How can you all be so gullible? This is different, Frank. Tell him. This asshole tried to sell us one week. We took the prick for three.
jokes.setup: Frank calling them gullible for the timeshare
jokes.punchline: Dennis proudly describing how they got more thoroughly scammed
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony. Dennis bragging about buying three weeks as proof they're smart. The profanity adds to the misplaced anger.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: How can you all be so gullible?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: This is different, Frank. Tell him. This asshole tried to sell us one week. We took the prick for three.

jokes.text: You think he didn't want to sell you as many weeks as possible?
jokes.setup: Dennis's pride in buying three weeks
jokes.punchline: Frank's obvious question that destroys their logic
jokes.explanation: Frank cutting through their delusion with basic logic. The obviousness makes it funnier that they missed it.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: You think he didn't want to sell you as many weeks as possible?

jokes.text: He went over there, probably saw some pretty serious carnage. He's gonna have some bad PTSD. If we trigger that, he'll be putty in our hands.
jokes.setup: Frank suggesting Ben as a target
jokes.punchline: His casual plan to exploit military trauma for profit
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about exploiting trauma. Frank's business-like approach to psychological manipulation is horrible but in character.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: He went over there, probably saw some pretty serious carnage. He's gonna have some bad PTSD. If we trigger that, he'll be putty in our hands.

jokes.text: You just got got, Frank. You played your hand a little too early, sucker.
jokes.setup: Frank giving them valuable information
jokes.punchline: Mac's misapplied business terminology while keeping Frank imprisoned
jokes.explanation: Mac using business talk to justify keeping Frank trapped. Good callback to their earlier confidence about not getting scammed.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: You just got got, Frank. You played your hand a little too early, sucker.

jokes.text: Boom! Sometimes, loud noises make the squirrels go in my head, and I don't fight in the ring anymore, but I still fight with the demons in Da'Maniac's head.
jokes.setup: Mac trying to trigger Ben's PTSD with loud noises
jokes.punchline: Da'Maniac's disturbing revelation about his own mental state
jokes.explanation: Da'Maniac's disturbing honesty about his mental health. 'Squirrels go in my head' is memorably bizarre. Shows how Mac's plan backfired.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Boom!

dialogue.speaker: Da'Maniac
dialogue.line: Sometimes, loud noises make the squirrels go in my head, and I don't fight in the ring anymore, but I still fight with the demons in Da'Maniac's head.

jokes.text: I have a 107 stress level. I'm sorry. I shouldn't be here. I-I have to go.
jokes.setup: Ben in business meeting
jokes.punchline: Using fake stress reading as excuse to leave
jokes.explanation: Ben taking the fake stress test seriously enough to affect his behavior

dialogue.line: I have a 107 stress level. I'm sorry. I shouldn't be here. I-I have to go.

jokes.text: I have a 107 stress level. I shouldn't be here. I-I have to go.
jokes.setup: Ben trying to leave the timeshare pitch
jokes.punchline: Him using the meaningless stress measurement as his excuse
jokes.explanation: Ben taking the fake measurement seriously. Good callback showing how the scams are affecting everyone across storylines.

dialogue.line: I have a 107 stress level. I shouldn't be here. I-I have to go.

jokes.text: Sold to the Maniac for $1,400. Really? You know why? 'Cause I can pay you in Invigaron.
jokes.setup: Da'Maniac buying the timeshare
jokes.punchline: His plan to pay with the berry scheme products instead of money
jokes.explanation: Da'Maniac's creative payment plan connecting the two schemes. Shows how the scams are creating a circular economy of nonsense.

dialogue.speaker: Da'Maniac
dialogue.line: Sold to the Maniac for $1,400. Really? You know why? 'Cause I can pay you in Invigaron.

jokes.text: Pyramid scheme and a time-share? We're diversifying our portfolio.
jokes.setup: Getting involved in multiple scams
jokes.punchline: Dennis using business jargon to justify bad decisions
jokes.explanation: Dennis's pseudo-sophisticated justification for multiple bad investments

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Pyramid scheme and a time-share? We're diversifying our portfolio.

jokes.text: I'd like a big room, if you don't mind. We're gonna have so much fun.
jokes.setup: Da'Maniac thinking he bought into timeshare
jokes.punchline: His assumption he's moving in with them
jokes.explanation: Da'Maniac's misunderstanding escalating their living situation problem

dialogue.speaker: Da'Maniac
dialogue.line: I'd like a big room, if you don't mind. We're gonna have so much fun.

jokes.text: We're gonna trigger Ben's PTSD, all right? We bust in like Navy SEALs, zip-tie his hands, bag over his head, and then we bash him till he buys.
jokes.setup: Mac's plan to exploit Ben
jokes.punchline: His escalation to basically torture tactics for a sales pitch
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation of their exploitation plan. Mac treating sales like military operations is absurd but disturbing.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: We're gonna trigger Ben's PTSD, all right? We bust in like Navy SEALs, zip-tie his hands, bag over his head, and then we bash him till he buys.

jokes.text: We broke in here, looking like Arabs, screaming at him. The guy's rock solid.
jokes.setup: Their plan to trigger Ben's PTSD
jokes.punchline: Dennis's casual racism and admission their torture didn't work
jokes.explanation: Shows how awful their plan was and how it failed. The casual racism is uncomfortable but reveals their terrible character.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: We broke in here, looking like Arabs, screaming at him. The guy's rock solid.

jokes.text: I actually had a good time during the war. I got to travel the world, uh, try new, exotic cuisines. Well, I'm a computer programmer.
jokes.setup: Everyone assuming Ben has combat PTSD
jokes.punchline: Ben revealing he's a computer programmer who enjoyed his deployment
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion of expectations. Ben's positive military experience and desk job completely undermines their evil plan.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I actually had a good time during the war. I got to travel the world, uh, try new, exotic cuisines. Well, I'm a computer programmer.

jokes.text: I just assumed that he went down there to pound off in the nighttime. Yeah, right, right. Yeah, me, too. Me, too.
jokes.setup: Realizing Frank might have had ulterior motives
jokes.punchline: Everyone's casual assumption that Frank goes to playgrounds to masturbate
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Frank's activities. Their casual acceptance of this horrible behavior shows how awful they all are.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I just assumed that he went down there to pound off in the nighttime.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yeah, right, right. Yeah, me, too.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Me, too.

jokes.text: Scam! This guy's a liar! You guys are getting scammed! Pyramid! Pyramid! Rape! Rape! Rape!
jokes.setup: Mac bursting into the Invigaron presentation
jokes.punchline: His escalation from 'scam' to screaming 'rape' repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Mac's manic escalation to increasingly dramatic warnings. The repetition of 'rape' is crude but shows his desperation.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Scam! This guy's a liar! You guys are getting scammed! Pyramid! Pyramid! Rape! Rape! Rape!

jokes.text: Look, you just, uh, sign this release form, pay a $200 penalty fee, your down line will shift up to me, and then you're free and clear.
jokes.setup: The gang thinking they can easily escape the MLM
jokes.punchline: The salesman calmly explaining how he profits from their escape too
jokes.explanation: Shows how the scam is designed to profit from every angle. Even 'escaping' costs money and benefits the scammer.

dialogue.line: Look, you just, uh, sign this release form, pay a $200 penalty fee, your down line will shift up to me, and then you're free and clear.

jokes.text: I'm just relieved you didn't see the old opt-out clause. Oh, no. Oh, crap, I've said too much.
jokes.setup: Harris 'accidentally' mentioning an opt-out clause
jokes.punchline: His obvious fake concern about revealing the 'secret' expensive exit
jokes.explanation: Classic reverse psychology sales tactic. Harris's 'accidental' revelation is obviously planned but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Harris
dialogue.line: I'm just relieved you didn't see the old opt-out clause. Oh, no. Oh, crap, I've said too much.

jokes.text: You also wrote 'faced' in the memo. Ha! Nice!
jokes.setup: Mac thinking they outsmarted the salesman
jokes.punchline: His excitement about a typo as if it proves their victory
jokes.explanation: Mac's misplaced pride in finding a typo while missing that he's still being scammed. Shows his poor priorities.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: You also wrote 'faced' in the memo. Ha! Nice!

jokes.text: This guy's the best salesman I got. He's already got 30 people under him. I got to thank you all so much for hooking me up with that army guy. I got his whole platoon selling for me.
jokes.setup: Da'Maniac supposedly being a victim of the scams
jokes.punchline: Revelation that he's actually the most successful seller
jokes.explanation: Perfect plot twist. Da'Maniac being the most successful salesman completely subverts expectations. Ben recruiting his whole platoon is the cherry on top.

dialogue.speaker: Harris
dialogue.line: This guy's the best salesman I got. He's already got 30 people under him. I got to thank you all so much for hooking me up with that army guy. I got his whole platoon selling for me.

jokes.text: You got got! You got got! Actually, he's in my down line. You just bought out, remember? Shoot!
jokes.setup: Da'Maniac celebrating getting rich from the scheme
jokes.punchline: Harris pointing out that Mac and Dennis bought out, so Da'Maniac gets nothing
jokes.explanation: Perfect comeuppance. Da'Maniac's victory is immediately deflated by the contract details. 'Shoot!' is perfectly understated.

dialogue.speaker: Da'Maniac
dialogue.line: You got got! You got got!

dialogue.speaker: Harris
dialogue.line: Actually, he's in my down line. You just bought out, remember? Shoot!

jokes.text: Actually, he's in my down line. You just bought out, remember?
jokes.setup: Dee thinking she's rich from Da'Maniac's success
jokes.punchline: Reminder that she paid to exit the scheme
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to exit clause - Dee loses her successful recruit

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Actually, he's in my down line. You just bought out, remember?

jokes.text: Hey! Hey, easy, easy, easy. I gotta get out of here! Get me out of here!
jokes.setup: Final check on Frank after all the schemes have unfolded
jokes.punchline: Frank still trapped in the coil, now with children around
jokes.explanation: Perfect closing callback. Frank's worst fear came true - he's still trapped and the kids found him. His panic is the final joke.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Hey! Hey, easy, easy, easy. I gotta get out of here! Get me out of here!
